ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for puffed rice as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for processed grain snacks is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of puffed rice is approximately 15 g (about 1 cup), as commonly consumed as a snack or breakfast cereal base.

nutrient_source.title: Puffed Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for puffed rice (cr_id F05090012). All per-100g values preserved from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Puffed rice GI is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). GI value of 82 (glucose=100 reference) is used; range reported in literature is 78–87 for plain puffed rice. Glycemic load per 15g serving calculated as (82 × 11.64g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 9.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100g) and moderate potassium (200 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Plain puffed rice is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to negligible sodium content (5 mg/100g); however, flavoured or salted commercial variants must be avoided as they may contain >400 mg sodium/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI ~82) and high glycemic load driven by 77.6 g carbohydrate/100g with only 2.8 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Puffed rice should be strictly limited or avoided by individuals with type 2 diabetes; the puffing process gelatinises starch granules causing rapid glucose absorption — if consumed, restrict to a small portion (~15 g) paired with protein and healthy fat to attenuate the glycemic spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low fat (3 g/100g), negligible sodium, and cholesterol-free, but refined high-GI carbohydrate with low fibre may contribute to dyslipidaemia and postprandial hyperglycaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain puffed rice is low in fat and sodium, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, its high glycemic index and low dietary fibre content (2.8 g/100g) mean regular consumption in large portions may adversely affect triglyceride levels and insulin sensitivity — whole grain alternatives are preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-GI refined carbohydrate with fibre below 3 g/100g and no anti-inflammatory micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Puffed rice provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit and its high glycemic load may promote systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis — limit consumption and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as walnuts, turmeric, or oily fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Puffed rice is a processed grain product made from white rice kernels that have been subjected to high heat and pressure, causing the starch granules to gelatinise and expand dramatically, producing light, airy, crispy white kernels with a mild toasted flavour. The puffing process significantly increases the glycemic index of the grain compared to cooked whole rice, resulting in a GI of approximately 82 (high). Per 100g, puffed rice provides 384 kcal, 8.5g protein, 3.0g fat, 77.6g carbohydrate, 0.6g sugar, 2.8g dietary fibre, and only 5mg sodium. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and very low in sodium. Puffed rice is widely consumed across South Asia (known as murmura, muri, pori, or mamra) as a street snack, breakfast cereal base, or ingredient in dishes such as bhel puri. It is also used globally as a breakfast cereal and in confectionery. Clinically, puffed rice is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its very high GI and glycemic load; it is suitable for hypertension management given its negligible sodium and moderate potassium content. Its low fat and cholesterol-free profile are cardiovascular-friendly, but the refined carbohydrate profile warrants moderation for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: Is puffed rice good for diabetes?
Puffed rice vs poha which is healthier?
Can I eat puffed rice on a diet?
Is puffed rice suitable for hypertension?

ingredient_synonyms.murmura: Hindi name for puffed rice, widely used in North India
ingredient_synonyms.muri: Bengali name for puffed rice, used in West Bengal and Bangladesh
ingredient_synonyms.pori: Tamil name for puffed rice, used in South India and Sri Lanka
ingredient_synonyms.rice puffs: alternate English descriptor used in commercial packaging
ingredient_synonyms.expanded rice: technical descriptor used in food science contexts

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05090012) with confidence 0.95. GI value of 82 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Food identity is unambiguous.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for puffed rice is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 20051, Rice, puffed) reports total fat 0.6g/100g with saturated fat 0.1g/100g as a cross-reference, but HPB value of 3.0g fat/100g is used as the primary approved source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food record. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; puffed rice is a plant-derived grain product containing no cholesterol. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (8.5), fat_g (3.0), and carbohydrate_g (77.6) are all non-zero. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 82 × available carbohydrate per 15g serving (77.6 × 0.15 = 11.64g) / 100 = 9.55, rounded to 9.6.
